channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Directive 8020 Promises Test Space Horror, But Has It Moved Too Far From Its Roots?
item.content:encoded:
We're only a few weeks away from the release of the latest of Supermassive Games' titles, Directive 8020. Once again, blurring the line between video game and interactive movie this new adventure will ask the question "What if The Thing took place in space?" Ahead of its release, we got to play a new short preview set in Chapter 4 of the game just as the spookiness is kicking off…

Directive 8020 follows a crew of Astronauts that was meant to travel to, but not land on, the planet Tau Ceti f. Due to events that take place at the beginning of the game, the crew of Astronauts finds themselves crash-landed on the planet, hoping to survive as a mysterious threat appears.

The preview that I got to play took place over two different time periods, one eighteen hours after the crash landing of the Cassiopeia, and one set in the 'present time', which seems to be an hour or so after landing, as a threat against the crew has been detected hiding in the vents.

Eighteen Hours Later

It's hard to fully understand from the context clues provided, but at this point, only a few members of the crew are remaining, and the strange shapeshifting threat has a dominant hold on the spaceship. Hazard lights are red, and these alien doppelgangers are patrolling the hallways searching for anything out of place.

In this section, I needed to stealthily make my way through a series of rooms, following the directions of another surviving crew member who was able to view my path on cameras.

With little in the way of a HUD directing me, it was very clear how open to interpretation directions like "Take the door ahead of you on the right" or "Leave through the window, not the door" are, as you're trying to keep your head on a swivel.

There were a few great moments where I'd make it to the end of an access tunnel or hop a barricade into a room for one of the creatures to walk in and get way too close to my hiding position, pause, and then turn around and give me some breathing room.

This segment was short-lived before I returned to the present time. No alarms were blaring, all of the crew were together and seemed… mostly happy. But a plan was being hatched to trap whoever was plotting against them.

Present Time

Joining the crew in the present, they were mid-commemoration of a crewmate, Tom Carter, who had seemingly died in a previous chapter. Here I got my first experience at regular decisions as well as 'Turning Points', what Directive 8020 is calling their key decision moments that will alter the course of the game.

These regular decisions might be the choice between believing someone or being skeptical, being objective driven, or pausing to offer some sympathy. With each selected option, I'd be notified that my character's traits had been updated. While unclear in the moment, it's likely that the more your character leans a certain way, the different options they'll have down the road.

The Turning Point decision that was made was whether lethal force would be authorized as part of their mission to detain the suspect. This felt like a big shift to be able to take in an adventure, especially as I know from earlier previews of the game that you'll end up in situations where you might be forced to pick between two people to shoot, one a clone of the other.

After decisions were made, I was led to a tense gameplay section, making my way through dark tunnels, slowly opening doors. While not much happened here outside of slowly walking, the tension was constantly building.

The tension remained, but gameplay pivoted as I was next in a large open area, having to skulk between obstacles so that the masked stalkers weren't able to find me. Here, it felt like I was playing a very standard survival horror title, pinging my scanner to watch the movements of my foe, and then moving at the right time to avoid his stare.

Once I'd completed the objective of opening the drawbridge, a QTE section kicked off as the stalker appeared in a doorway in front of me (somehow?) and we fought. This is where my earlier Turning Point decision came into play, as the stalker was shot twice mid-combat.

Space Horror Is There, But Doesn't Feel As Supermassive

It's clear from even this small presentation that the vibes of Directive 8020 are going to be immaculate. I do fear that with more involved gameplay, it leans a bit more away from the cinematic moments of Supermassive Games' previous titles.

One of my favorite aspects of playing these titles is getting friends together to play them as really long movie nights. If all a player needs to do is steer or press buttons for quick time events, the barrier to entry is quite low. If you're now needing to navigate large levels, use radar and remote hacking features to distract your enemies, and use a stun baton to shake off enemies, I fear my less video game inclined friends won't take to it in the same manner.

With less than a month until the full release, I've been very impressed by the gameplay that I have seen, if a little bit fearful, and I'm definitely intrigued about the story. I can't wait to see how the final experience is once I've experienced the whole thing.

Directive 8020 was previewed with a code provided by the publisher over 1 hour - All images were taken during the preview process

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/directive-8020-promises-test-space-horror-but-has-it-moved-too-far-from-its-roots
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Vampire: The Masquerade - Bloodlines 2 Gets "Largest Update to Date"
item.content:encoded:
Paradox and The Chinese Room have released Vampire: The Masquerade - Bloodlines 2's Spring Update, which the two studios say is the game's "largest update to date", and it's got lots of changes in store.

According to the two studios, the update, which is out now, contains "several quality-of-life features" requested by the Bloodlines 2 community, as well as a big melee combat update that adds something the game should arguably have had from launch.

If you've played Bloodlines 2, you'll know how frustrating it is that your character can't just pick up melee weapons and use them in combat, but now they can, and each of the weapons will "play differently and add alternate utility" to your unarmed attacks. Being unarmed is still advantageous in certain situations, though.

Other combat changes include the ability to attack while you're targeting an ability, as well as a new Guard feature to block enemy attacks. A series of tutorial collectibles are now available to find throughout Bloodlines 2 to help you get used to these new mechanics.

As well as the combat overhaul, you'll now find a new Save Last Checkpoint feature that essentially serves as a manual save, allowing you to go back and redo decisions you weren't happy with. That's in addition to the ability to fast-travel to Fabien's informant locations.

Additionally, several quests that were mainline missions have now been turned into sidequests, giving you "more choice on repeat playthroughs on what quests to complete or skip", according to The Chinese Room.

You can read the full changelog for Bloodlines 2's Spring Update right here. It's unlikely to reverse the game's dismal financial fortunes, nor those of its publisher thanks to its dire performance, but hey, it's something.

You can check out Vampire: The Masquerade - Bloodlines 2 right now on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S. The game's Spring Update is out now on all platforms.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/vampire-masquerade-bloodlines-2-spring-update
